Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|34.4|N|135|48|12.6|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Ueyama Kofun'''|植山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Gojono neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2002 with the area under protection expanded in 2003..<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163018|title=植山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It was said to be the joint tomb of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] and her son Prince Takeda before they were reburied in the Yamada Takatsuka Kofun the Isonagatani Kofun cluster.
==Overview== The Ueyama Kofun is located at the western end of the hills extending from Amakashi-no-oka, at the southern end of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]]. The surrounding area is home to a number of important ancient tombs from the 6th and 7th centuries, including the [[Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)| Maruyama Kofun]] located about 500 meters west of Ueyama Kofun and [[Shōbuike Kofun]] located about 700 meters southeast. The Ueyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}-style rectangular Kofun measuring 40 meters east-to-west and 27 meters north-to-south and between three and six meters in height, although it may have originally been an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular ''kofun''. It was built by partly carving out the southern slope of a natural hill. The east, west, and north of the mound are surrounded by a U-shaped moat. The tumulus is a twin-chamber tomb as it has two horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, one on the east side and one on the west side. The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka region]] from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].
The east burial chamber has a length of 13 meters and opens to the south. It has an antechamber length of 6.5 meters, a width of 3.0 to 3.2 meters. The ceiling stone that has been lost but the remaining ceiling height is 3.1 meters. It has an entrance passageway length of 6.5 meters, a width of 1.9 meters, and a height of 2.2 meters. A hollowed-out house-shaped Sarcophagus made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]] was placed in the antechamber. In the drainage ditch, gilt bronze ornamental metal fittings from horse harnesses and a crystal three-ringed ball hilt from an iron sword were found. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The coffin is lost, but fragments of Aso tuff were found in the burial chamber, suggesting the possibility that a Sarcophagus was placed there. In the center of the burial chamber, there is a stone support that serves as the door that separates the burial chamber from the passageway. [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] was excavated..<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The location and configuration of this tumulus correspond to an account in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', which stated that Empress Suiko was buried in the tomb of her son, Prince Takeda, in 628. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' adds that the remains were later relocated to a larger tomb in Isonagatani, [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Yoshino Line|Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

={{ill|Q80793203|WD=Q80793203}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|34.4|N|135|48|12.6|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Ueyama Kofun'''|植山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Gojono neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2002 with the area under protection expanded in 2003..<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163018|title=植山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It was said to be the joint tomb of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] and her son Prince Takeda before they were reburied in the Yamada Takatsuka Kofun the Isonagatani Kofun cluster.
==Overview== The Ueyama Kofun is located at the western end of the hills extending from Amakashi-no-oka, at the southern end of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]]. The surrounding area is home to a number of important ancient tombs from the 6th and 7th centuries, including the [[Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)| Maruyama Kofun]] located about 500 meters west of Ueyama Kofun and [[Shōbuike Kofun]] located about 700 meters southeast. The Ueyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}-style rectangular Kofun measuring 40 meters east-to-west and 27 meters north-to-south and between three and six meters in height, although it may have originally been an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular ''kofun''. It was built by partly carving out the southern slope of a natural hill. The east, west, and north of the mound are surrounded by a U-shaped moat. The tumulus is a twin-chamber tomb as it has two horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, one on the east side and one on the west side. The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka region]] from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].
The east burial chamber has a length of 13 meters and opens to the south. It has an antechamber length of 6.5 meters, a width of 3.0 to 3.2 meters. The ceiling stone that has been lost but the remaining ceiling height is 3.1 meters. It has an entrance passageway length of 6.5 meters, a width of 1.9 meters, and a height of 2.2 meters. A hollowed-out house-shaped Sarcophagus made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]] was placed in the antechamber. In the drainage ditch, gilt bronze ornamental metal fittings from horse harnesses and a crystal three-ringed ball hilt from an iron sword were found. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The coffin is lost, but fragments of Aso tuff were found in the burial chamber, suggesting the possibility that a Sarcophagus was placed there. In the center of the burial chamber, there is a stone support that serves as the door that separates the burial chamber from the passageway. [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] was excavated..<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The location and configuration of this tumulus correspond to an account in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', which stated that Empress Suiko was buried in the tomb of her son, Prince Takeda, in 628. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' adds that the remains were later relocated to a larger tomb in Isonagatani, [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Yoshino Line|Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

It was built by partly carving out the southern slope of a natural hill. The east, west, and north of the mound are surrounded by a U-shaped moat. The tumulus is a twin-chamber tomb as it has two horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, one on the east side and one on the west side. The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka region]] from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].
The east burial chamber has a length of 13 meters and opens to the south. It has an antechamber length of 6.5 meters, a width of 3.0 to 3.2 meters. The ceiling stone that has been lost but the remaining ceiling height is 3.1 meters. It has an entrance passageway length of 6.5 meters, a width of 1.9 meters, and a height of 2.2 meters. A hollowed-out house-shaped Sarcophagus made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] from [[:en:Mount Aso|Mount Aso]] was placed in the antechamber. In the drainage ditch, gilt bronze ornamental metal fittings from horse harnesses and a crystal three-ringed ball hilt from an iron sword were found. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The coffin is lost, but fragments of Aso tuff were found in the burial chamber, suggesting the possibility that a Sarcophagus was placed there. In the center of the burial chamber, there is a stone support that serves as the door that separates the burial chamber from the passageway. [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] was excavated..<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The location and configuration of this tumulus correspond to an account in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', which stated that Empress Suiko was buried in the tomb of her son, Prince Takeda, in 628. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' adds that the remains were later relocated to a larger tomb in Isonagatani, [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Yoshino Line|Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

It was built by partly carving out the southern slope of a natural hill. The east, west, and north of the mound are surrounded by a U-shaped moat. The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka region}} from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.
The east burial chamber has a length of 13 meters and opens to the south. It has an antechamber length of 6.5 meters, a width of 3.0 to 3.2 meters. The ceiling stone that has been lost but the remaining ceiling height is 3.1 meters. It has an entrance passageway length of 6.5 meters, a width of 1.9 meters, and a height of 2.2 meters. A hollowed-out house-shaped Sarcophagus made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso}} was placed in the antechamber. In the drainage ditch, gilt bronze ornamental metal fittings from horse harnesses and a crystal three-ringed ball hilt from an iron sword were found. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The coffin is lost, but fragments of Aso tuff were found in the burial chamber, suggesting the possibility that a Sarcophagus was placed there. In the center of the burial chamber, there is a stone support that serves as the door that separates the burial chamber from the passageway. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} was excavated..<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The location and configuration of this tumulus correspond to an account in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', which stated that Empress Suiko was buried in the tomb of her son, Prince Takeda, in 628. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' adds that the remains were later relocated to a larger tomb in Isonagatani, {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Ueyama Kofun'''|植山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Gojono neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2002 with the area under protection expanded in 2003..<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163018|title=植山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It was said to be the joint tomb of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} and her son Prince Takeda before they were reburied in the Yamada Takatsuka Kofun the Isonagatani Kofun cluster.
==Overview== The Ueyama Kofun is located at the western end of the hills extending from Amakashi-no-oka, at the southern end of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin}}. The surrounding area is home to a number of important ancient tombs from the 6th and 7th centuries, including the [[Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)| Maruyama Kofun]] located about 500 meters west of Ueyama Kofun and [[Shōbuike Kofun]] located about 700 meters southeast. The Ueyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}-style rectangular Kofun measuring 40 meters east-to-west and 27 meters north-to-south and between three and six meters in height, although it may have originally been an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular ''kofun''. It was built by partly carving out the southern slope of a natural hill. The east, west, and north of the mound are surrounded by a U-shaped moat. The tumulus is a twin-chamber tomb as it has two horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, one on the east side and one on the west side. The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka region}} from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.
The east burial chamber has a length of 13 meters and opens to the south. It has an antechamber length of 6.5 meters, a width of 3.0 to 3.2 meters. The ceiling stone that has been lost but the remaining ceiling height is 3.1 meters. It has an entrance passageway length of 6.5 meters, a width of 1.9 meters, and a height of 2.2 meters. A hollowed-out house-shaped Sarcophagus made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso}} was placed in the antechamber. In the drainage ditch, gilt bronze ornamental metal fittings from horse harnesses and a crystal three-ringed ball hilt from an iron sword were found. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The coffin is lost, but fragments of Aso tuff were found in the burial chamber, suggesting the possibility that a Sarcophagus was placed there. In the center of the burial chamber, there is a stone support that serves as the door that separates the burial chamber from the passageway. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} was excavated..<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The location and configuration of this tumulus correspond to an account in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', which stated that Empress Suiko was buried in the tomb of her son, Prince Takeda, in 628. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' adds that the remains were later relocated to a larger tomb in Isonagatani, {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka region}} from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.
The east burial chamber has a length of 13 meters and opens to the south. It has an antechamber length of 6.5 meters, a width of 3.0 to 3.2 meters. The ceiling stone that has been lost but the remaining ceiling height is 3.1 meters. It has an entrance passageway length of 6.5 meters, a width of 1.9 meters, and a height of 2.2 meters. A hollowed-out house-shaped Sarcophagus made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso}} was placed in the antechamber. In the drainage ditch, gilt bronze ornamental metal fittings from horse harnesses and a crystal three-ringed ball hilt from an iron sword were found. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The coffin is lost, but fragments of Aso tuff were found in the burial chamber, suggesting the possibility that a Sarcophagus was placed there. In the center of the burial chamber, there is a stone support that serves as the door that separates the burial chamber from the passageway. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} was excavated..<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The location and configuration of this tumulus correspond to an account in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', which stated that Empress Suiko was buried in the tomb of her son, Prince Takeda, in 628. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' adds that the remains were later relocated to a larger tomb in Isonagatani, {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Ueyama Kofun'''|植山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Gojono neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2002 with the area under protection expanded in 2003..<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163018|title=植山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It was said to be the joint tomb of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} and her son Prince Takeda before they were reburied in the Yamada Takatsuka Kofun the Isonagatani Kofun cluster.
==Overview== The Ueyama Kofun is located at the western end of the hills extending from Amakashi-no-oka, at the southern end of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin}}. The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka region}} from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.

The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka region}} from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.
The east burial chamber has a length of 13 meters and opens to the south. It has an antechamber length of 6.5 meters, a width of 3.0 to 3.2 meters. The ceiling stone that has been lost but the remaining ceiling height is 3.1 meters. It has an entrance passageway length of 6.5 meters, a width of 1.9 meters, and a height of 2.2 meters. A hollowed-out house-shaped Sarcophagus made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso}} was placed in the antechamber. In the drainage ditch, gilt bronze ornamental metal fittings from horse harnesses and a crystal three-ringed ball hilt from an iron sword were found. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The coffin is lost, but fragments of Aso tuff were found in the burial chamber, suggesting the possibility that a Sarcophagus was placed there. In the center of the burial chamber, there is a stone support that serves as the door that separates the burial chamber from the passageway. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} was excavated..<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The location and configuration of this tumulus correspond to an account in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', which stated that Empress Suiko was buried in the tomb of her son, Prince Takeda, in 628. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' adds that the remains were later relocated to a larger tomb in Isonagatani, {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Ueyama Kofun'''|植山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Gojono neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara|1=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2002 with the area under protection expanded in 2003..<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163018|title=植山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It was said to be the joint tomb of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} and her son Prince Takeda before they were reburied in the Yamada Takatsuka Kofun the Isonagatani Kofun cluster.
==Overview== The Ueyama Kofun is located at the western end of the hills extending from Amakashi-no-oka, at the southern end of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin|1=Nara Basin}}. The surrounding area is home to a number of important ancient tombs from the 6th and 7th centuries, including the [[Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)| Maruyama Kofun]] located about 500 meters west of Ueyama Kofun and [[Shōbuike Kofun]] located about 700 meters southeast. It was built by partly carving out the southern slope of a natural hill. The east, west, and north of the mound are surrounded by a U-shaped moat. The tumulus is a twin-chamber tomb as it has two horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, one on the east side and one on the west side. The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka region|1=Asuka, Nara}} from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}.

The ceiling stone that has been lost but the remaining ceiling height is 3.1 meters. It has an entrance passageway length of 6.5 meters, a width of 1.9 meters, and a height of 2.2 meters. A hollowed-out house-shaped Sarcophagus made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso|1=Mount Aso}} was placed in the antechamber. In the drainage ditch, gilt bronze ornamental metal fittings from horse harnesses and a crystal three-ringed ball hilt from an iron sword were found. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The coffin is lost, but fragments of Aso tuff were found in the burial chamber, suggesting the possibility that a Sarcophagus was placed there. In the center of the burial chamber, there is a stone support that serves as the door that separates the burial chamber from the passageway. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} was excavated..<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The location and configuration of this tumulus correspond to an account in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', which stated that Empress Suiko was buried in the tomb of her son, Prince Takeda, in 628. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' adds that the remains were later relocated to a larger tomb in Isonagatani, {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station|1=Okadera Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line|1=Yoshino Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The Ueyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}-style rectangular Kofun measuring 40 meters east-to-west and 27 meters north-to-south and between three and six meters in height, although it may have originally been an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular ''kofun''. It was built by partly carving out the southern slope of a natural hill. The east, west, and north of the mound are surrounded by a U-shaped moat. The tumulus is a twin-chamber tomb as it has two horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, one on the east side and one on the west side. The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka region|1=Asuka, Nara}} from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}.
The east burial chamber has a length of 13 meters and opens to the south. It has an antechamber length of 6.5 meters, a width of 3.0 to 3.2 meters. The ceiling stone that has been lost but the remaining ceiling height is 3.1 meters. It has an entrance passageway length of 6.5 meters, a width of 1.9 meters, and a height of 2.2 meters. A hollowed-out house-shaped Sarcophagus made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso|1=Mount Aso}} was placed in the antechamber. In the drainage ditch, gilt bronze ornamental metal fittings from horse harnesses and a crystal three-ringed ball hilt from an iron sword were found. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The coffin is lost, but fragments of Aso tuff were found in the burial chamber, suggesting the possibility that a Sarcophagus was placed there. In the center of the burial chamber, there is a stone support that serves as the door that separates the burial chamber from the passageway. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} was excavated..<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The location and configuration of this tumulus correspond to an account in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', which stated that Empress Suiko was buried in the tomb of her son, Prince Takeda, in 628. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' adds that the remains were later relocated to a larger tomb in Isonagatani, {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station|1=Okadera Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line|1=Yoshino Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]]{{template-inline|Ueyama Kofun}} *[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/soshiki/1058/gyomu/3/2/1/3707.html Kashihara City home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://kashihara-kanko.or.jp/spot/detail.php?sid=S00049 Kashihara City tourist information] {{in lang|ja}} Ueyama Kofun

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Rectangular Kofun|ja|長方形墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Ueyama Kofun | native_name =植山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 植山古墳 出土品.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Grave goods from the Ueyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ueyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.47622222|N|135.8035|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Kashihara|WD=Q496319|1=Kashihara, Nara}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.6th-7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|Q80793203|WD=Q80793203|1=Rectangular Kofun}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|34.4|N|135|48|12.6|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Ueyama Kofun'''|植山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Gojono neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara|1=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2002 with the area under protection expanded in 2003..<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163018|title=植山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It was said to be the joint tomb of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} and her son Prince Takeda before they were reburied in the Yamada Takatsuka Kofun the Isonagatani Kofun cluster.
==Overview== The Ueyama Kofun is located at the western end of the hills extending from Amakashi-no-oka, at the southern end of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin|1=Nara Basin}}. The construction method and shape of the tumulus are typical of the ancient tombs of the {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka region|1=Asuka, Nara}} from the end of the 6th century to the first half of the 7th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was robbed before the modern period, and a number of the stones used in its construction, including the monolithic ceiling stone for the east burial chamber, which was repurposed as a water basin during the construction of [[Kashihara Shrine]] in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}.

A hollowed-out house-shaped Sarcophagus made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|WD=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso|1=Mount Aso}} was placed in the antechamber. In the drainage ditch, gilt bronze ornamental metal fittings from horse harnesses and a crystal three-ringed ball hilt from an iron sword were found. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The western burial chamber is also 13 meters long and opens to the south. It is 5.2 by 2.5 meters, and although most of the ceiling stones have also been lost, it is 4.5 meters high. The passageway is 7.8 meters long, 2.3 meters wide and 2.0 meters high. The coffin is lost, but fragments of Aso tuff were found in the burial chamber, suggesting the possibility that a Sarcophagus was placed there. In the center of the burial chamber, there is a stone support that serves as the door that separates the burial chamber from the passageway. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} was excavated..<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The ''[[Kojiki]]'' adds that the remains were later relocated to a larger tomb in Isonagatani, {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}. .<ref name="Shiseki"/>
